title: OpenAI may have made a fatal misstep in copyright fight with news orgs
gist: The NYT-led news plaintiffs allege OpenAI concealed for two years that it could search ChatGPT logs and had already done so, deleted billions of logs, and over-redacted samples, seeking severe sanctions that could cripple OpenAI's fair-use defense in the landmark copyright case.

insights.claim: OpenAI allegedly pretended from the earliest stages that it lacked the technical ability to search large anonymized samples of ChatGPT logs, when it had already conducted such searches before litigation began.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: If proven, the concealment converts a technical discovery fight into a credibility problem that could decide the case and set precedent for all AI copyright suits.
insights.quote: OpenAI was willing and able to search its output logs—when it benefitted OpenAI

insights.claim: OpenAI allegedly held de-identified samples of 10 million and 78 million logs it never disclosed over two years, forcing plaintiffs to spend eight months on a heavily redacted 20 million-log 'sandbox' sample.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: The scale of withheld evidence directly bears on whether regurgitation of copyrighted content was substantial, the crux of market-harm and fair use.

insights.claim: OpenAI used AI to make 19 billion redactions to the 20 million-log sample, so many that the court found the sample 'unusable.'
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Over-redaction that renders evidence unusable is the specific conduct plaintiffs want sanctioned by barring OpenAI from using that sample.
insights.locator: Court says OpenAI sample is unusable

insights.claim: OpenAI's witness testified the company 'decided' that complying with the court's preservation order to retain all chats 'would be hard' and thus took no steps to do so, allegedly deleting or compressing billions of logs.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Willful spoliation invites an adverse-inference jury instruction, one of the most damaging sanctions available.
insights.quote: OpenAI thought about complying with the Court's Preservation Order, but then decided not to

insights.claim: Whether training on copyrighted content is fair use will likely turn on whether news organizations can establish market harm, which the withheld logs are central to proving.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Market harm is the decisive fair-use factor for the whole AI-training legal landscape; this evidence fight is a proxy for the industry's exposure.
insights.quote: Whether training on copyrighted content is fair use will likely depend on whether news organizations can establish market harms
insights.locator: If the court agrees that OpenAI's misconduct was egregious

insights.claim: Plaintiffs added claims against Microsoft, framing the suit as streamlined and strengthened rather than weakened despite dropping some claims that OpenAI cites as evidence the case is crumbling.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Microsoft's inclusion widens the liability net to a deep-pocketed defendant and signals the plaintiffs are digging in, not retreating.
insights.quote: Microsoft and OpenAI stole millions of The Times's copyrighted works to compete with our products and illegally enrich themselves
insights.locator: However, last month, NYT spokesperson Graham James

insights.claim: News plaintiffs seek severe sanctions barring OpenAI from using the 20 million sample, a finding that withheld logs included substantial regurgitation, and a jury instruction on the deleted logs.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: The requested remedies would effectively concede infringement facts to the jury, a potentially fatal blow to OpenAI's defense.
